Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore as a private limited company on 23 November 2016.
At the time of its incorporation on 23 November 2016, all 500,000 initial shares in Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd were held by Mr Zheng Chunmu, also known as Allen.
Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd is in the business of renting industrial machinery.
From 23 November 2016, Ms Zhu Yimin was recorded as a director of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd.
From 23 November 2016 to 27 May 2018, Ms Zhu Yimin was the sole recorded director of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd.

On 22 March 2018, Express Corporate Services Pte Ltd issued a tax invoice to Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd charging a fee of $1,200 for 'one year [of] nominee director fee' from 23 November 2017 to 22 November 2018.
According to Ms Zhu, in 2016, Mr Zheng Chunmu requested her services to incorporate Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd.

According to Ms Zhu, Express Corporate Services Pte Ltd provided Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd with accountancy, secretarial and tax services for varying periods from 2018 to 2021, and also provided nominee director services because Mr Zheng was a foreigner.
According to Ms Zhu, Express Corporate Services Pte Ltd provided Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd with accountancy, secretarial and tax services for varying periods from 2018 to 2021.
Mr Xu Jinsong was a director of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd from 28 May 2018 to 14 January 2019 and from 15 August 2019 to date.

Mr Xu Jinsong was a director of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd from 28 May 2018 to 14 January 2019, and Ms Zhu Yimin was a director from 23 November 2016 to 25 September 2019 and from 30 July 2020 to 1 January 2021.
Mr Xu Jinsong held 50% of the shares in Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd after the 13 April 2018 Cooperation Agreement and transfer of 250,000 shares.

On 13 April 2018, Mr Zheng Chunmu and Mr Xu Jinsong entered into a Cooperation Agreement under which Mr Zheng agreed to sell 50% of the shares in Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd to Mr Xu for $100,000, and Mr Xu agreed to provide $200,000 of interest-free working capital for Pioneer's use.
A total of 250,000 shares of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd's initial 500,000 shares were accordingly transferred to Mr Xu Jinsong.
Around 3 July 2018, Mr Xu Jinsong and Mr Zheng Chunmu, on behalf of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd, took out an SME Working Capital Loan of $259,000 from United Overseas Bank.
Around 3 July 2018, Mr Xu Jinsong and Mr Zheng Chunmu, on behalf of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd, took out a Bizmoney Loan of $100,000 from United Overseas Bank.
Mr Xu Jinsong was the personal guarantor of both loans.

On 18 October 2018, an email was sent to 'Jason' in Pioneer from Xiao Ru, whom Ms Zhu testified was a staff of ECS, seeking information about bank statements, loan details, the recipients of certain payments and the purpose of such payments.

On 14 January 2019, a Withdrawal Agreement was signed under which, from that date, Mr Zheng Chunmu would control all the business activities of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd and own all profits and losses, and Mr Xu Jinsong would resign as Pioneer’s director and would not be 'legally responsible', while retaining his 50% equity interest.
Ms Zhu Yimin was the sole director of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd from 15 January 2019 to 14 August 2019.
Mr Xu Jinsong was a director of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd from 15 August 2019 to date.
A purchase order numbered PO180429 dated 29 April 2018 was allegedly signed by an employee of Pioneer, Gao Jie.
On 14 May 2018, Gao Jie was appointed as Admin Manager of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd, and the letter of appointment was signed by Ms Zhu Yimin.
Around April 2018, Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd made a payment of $20,000 through Mr Zheng to Pan Yong.
The Claimants alleged that Ms Zhu issued a cheque for $1,070 in favour of WKS Welding Products Pte Ltd on behalf of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd.
The Claimants alleged that a certificate certifying that Lin Duan held 30,000 shares in Pioneer bore a signature that appeared to be similar to Ms Zhu's.
The letter of authorisation from Pioneer for Lin Duan to collect cheques was signed by Mr Zheng, not Ms Zhu.

On 25 September 2019, in a WhatsApp exchange, Ms Zhu asked Mr Xu for bank statements and accounting documents if he wanted her assistance with a power of attorney for debt collection, and said she could not believe the assertion that the other side took the money.
On 11 October 2019, Mr Xu stated in a WhatsApp message to Ms Zhu that Mr Zheng, referred to as 'Allen', was the actual controller responsible for the operation and profit and loss.
Around 8 July 2020, Ms Zhu told Mr Xu that she was a 'nominee director', provided '[j]ust service', was 'not [the] real boss', and said 'I am different from you two [ie, Mr Xu and Mr Zheng]'.
Ms Zhu Yimin was a director of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd from 23 November 2016 to 25 September 2019.
Ms Zhu Yimin was a director of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd from 30 July 2020 to 1 January 2021.
Ms Zhu Yimin was a director of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd from 30 July 2020 to 1 January 2021.
On 18 December 2019, Express Corporate Services Pte Ltd issued an invoice to Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd for $2,660 described as fees for accounting services.
On 19 December 2018, Express Corporate Services Pte Ltd sent a chaser email to Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd regarding payment of an invoice.
On 9 July 2021, Express Corporate Services Pte Ltd sent a chaser email to Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd regarding payment of an invoice.
On 20 July 2021, Express Corporate Services Pte Ltd sent a chaser email to Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd regarding payment of an invoice.
On 28 November 2018, Express Corporate Services Pte Ltd issued an invoice to Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd that included an $80 charge for Ms Zhu going to the bank to sign as a director.
As at 25 November 2022, the ACRA profile for Pioneer only listed Mr Zheng Chunmu and Mr Xu Jinsong as shareholders.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on the conduct and roles of the individuals involved before the dispute, Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd could recover specific sums and obtain orders to account in relation to cheques, bank withdrawals, purchase orders, payments to third parties, share issuances, cheque collections, and financial statements associated with its operations.
Party_Details.Rule: In company disputes concerning directors and persons acting in management, liability to account or repay typically depends on whether, under general company law principles, the person exercised control or authority over the relevant corporate acts and funds, and whether the claimant can prove the existence and quantum of the impugned transactions and the person’s involvement in them.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute records Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd was owned in equal shares by Mr Zheng Chunmu and Mr Xu Jinsong after April 2018, that Mr Zheng was described in communications as the actual controller responsible for operations and profit and loss, and that cheques and authorisation letters relating to payments and cheque collections were signed in the names of Mr Zheng and Mr Xu rather than in the name of Ms Zhu Yimin. Express Corporate Services Pte Ltd provided corporate and accounting services, and Ms Zhu was recorded as a director and was invoiced as a nominee director, while internal communications show her requesting bank statements and accounting documents from Mr Xu, indicating that she did not hold direct access to Pioneer’s bank records. These pre-dispute facts frame the allocation of operational control and signature authority Pioneer’s claims for recovery and accounting.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court analysed whether Ms Zhu Yimin functioned as a managing director or only as a nominee director and concluded, based on WhatsApp messages, invoices, and ACRA records, that she was a nominee director with a non-executive role. The judge relied on communications where Mr Xu described Mr Zheng as the actual controller responsible for operations and profit and loss, and on evidence that Ms Zhu and her corporate services firm had to request bank statements and payment information from Mr Xu. The court compared signatures on cheques and documents and found that the cheques forming the basis of the $276,876.65 claim were signed by Mr Zheng and Mr Xu, not by Ms Zhu, and that there was no evidence that she authorised or was involved in those cheques. For the alleged unexplained withdrawals of $744,737.42, the court noted the absence of any evidential basis in Mr Xu’s affidavits or supporting documents linking Ms Zhu to such withdrawals, and accepted Ms Zhu’s evidence that only Mr Zheng and Mr Xu were signatories for Pioneer’s bank transactions. Regarding the purchase order PO180429, the court observed inconsistencies in the claimants’ positions about the signature, the lack of proof that Gao Jie signed it, and the absence of evidence of Ms Zhu’s involvement, and therefore rejected the claim. The court found no evidence connecting Ms Zhu to the $20,000 payment to Pan Yong or to any improper purpose, and similarly no evidence that she issued or signed the $1,070 cheque to WKS Welding Products Pte Ltd. On the dealings with Lin Duan, the court accepted that a share certificate bore a signature similar to Ms Zhu’s but noted that ACRA records did not show Lin Duan as a shareholder and that the authorisation letter for cheque collection was signed by Mr Zheng, not Ms Zhu; there was no evidence of wrongdoing or of Ms Zhu’s role in any cheque collection. For the period 30 July 2020 to 1 January 2021, the court accepted Ms Zhu’s explanation that she was mistakenly filed as a director by her assistant and that she corrected the error with ACRA. On the request for financial statements for FY 2018 and 2019, the court applied Ezion Holdings Ltd v Teras Cargo Transport Pte Ltd, holding that the statutory provisions in the Companies Act create offences for failure to lay accounts at an AGM but do not support a civil claim to compel production of accounts outside an AGM, and further noted that there was no evidence that AGMs had been held or that Ms Zhu alone bore responsibility, given that Mr Xu was also a director for part of the relevant period. The court considered the general duties of directors, including nominee directors, as set out in authorities such as Prima Bulkship and BIT Baltic, but found that the claimants had not adduced evidence of any specific acts by Ms Zhu that would amount to a breach of those duties. The court also noted that ACRA had informed Mr Xu that his complaints did not disclose wrongdoing under the Companies Act. Consequently, all of the claimants’ heads of claim were dismissed. On the counterclaim, the court held that the invoice for $2,660 was issued by Express Corporate Services Pte Ltd, not by Ms Zhu personally, and that there was no evidence from Pioneer acknowledging the debt or evidence of services rendered, so the counterclaim was dismissed. For the alleged additional interest on a UOB loan, the court found that any loss would be that of Express Corporate Services Pte Ltd, that there was no evidence linking the increased interest rate or refinancing difficulties to the present suit, and dismissed that counterclaim as well. Costs were awarded to Ms Zhu on the claim and to Mr Xu on the counterclaim, with directions for submissions on quantum.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Xu Jinsong

Facts.Text: Mr Xu Jinsong was a director of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd from 28 May 2018 to 14 January 2019, and again from 15 August 2019 to date.

From 28 May 2018 to 14 January 2019, Mr Xu Jinsong was a director of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd; Ms Zhu Yimin was the sole director only until 27 May 2018 and again from 15 January 2019 to 14 August 2019.
From 15 August 2019 to date, Mr Xu Jinsong was again recorded as a director of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd.
Mr Xu Jinsong is the 50% shareholder of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd.

On 13 April 2018, Mr Zheng Chunmu and Mr Xu Jinsong entered into a Cooperation Agreement under which Mr Zheng agreed to sell 50% of the shares in Pioneer for $100,000 and Mr Xu would provide $200,000 of interest-free working capital for Pioneer's use.
A total of 250,000 shares of Pioneer’s initial 500,000 shares were transferred to Mr Xu Jinsong.
Around 3 July 2018, Mr Xu Jinsong and Mr Zheng Chunmu, on behalf of Pioneer, took out an SME Working Capital Loan of $259,000 from United Overseas Bank.
Around 3 July 2018, Mr Xu Jinsong and Mr Zheng Chunmu, on behalf of Pioneer, took out a Bizmoney Loan of $100,000 from United Overseas Bank.
Mr Xu Jinsong was the personal guarantor of both loans.

On 14 January 2019, a Withdrawal Agreement was signed. It provided that from that date, Mr Zheng would control all the business activities of Pioneer and own all profits and losses of Pioneer, while Mr Xu would resign as Pioneer’s director and would not be legally responsible, but would retain his 50% equity in Pioneer.
On 14 January 2019, in connection with the Withdrawal Agreement, Mr Xu agreed to resign as Pioneer’s director while retaining his 50% equity in Pioneer.
On 20 November 2019, Mr Xu brought MC/MC 17007/2019 against Mr Zheng for the sum of around $55,000, for alleged breaches of the Withdrawal Agreement by Mr Zheng.
On 25 September 2019, in a WhatsApp exchange, Mr Xu asked Ms Zhu to help with a power of attorney for debt collection and was told to bring bank statements and accounting documents.
On 11 October 2019, in a WhatsApp message to Ms Zhu, Mr Xu wrote that Allen, referring to Mr Zheng, was the actual controller responsible for the operation and profit and loss of Pioneer.
On 22 December 2018, in a WhatsApp exchange, Ms Zhu said '[y]ou quit'.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute conduct and agreements, Xu Jinsong could obtain repayment and accounting from Zhu Yimin in relation to company cheques, withdrawals, purchase orders, payments, share issuances, cheque collections, and financial statements connected to Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: In disputes between shareholders or directors and another individual associated with a company, recovery of sums or orders to account generally depends on proof that the individual controlled or authorised the relevant transactions and that the claimant can establish the existence, amount, and linkage of those transactions to that individual under applicable company and contract law principles.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute materials Xu Jinsong became a 50% shareholder and director of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd under a Cooperation Agreement with Zheng Chunmu, personally guaranteed bank loans obtained for the company, and later signed a Withdrawal Agreement under which Zheng would control business activities and bear profits and losses while Xu resigned as director but retained his equity. Communications from Xu to Zhu described Zheng as the actual controller responsible for operations and profit and loss, and documents relating to cheques and authorisations were signed in the names of Xu and Zheng rather than Zhu. These facts Xu and Zheng held operational and financial authority in relation to the transactions that later formed the subject of Xu’s claims against Zhu.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court considered Xu Jinsong’s position as a 50% shareholder and director of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd, his role in entering into the Cooperation Agreement and Withdrawal Agreement with Zheng Chunmu, and his personal guarantees of the company’s bank loans. It noted that Xu’s own communications described Zheng as the actual controller responsible for operations and profit and loss. In assessing Xu’s claims against Zhu Yimin, the court examined the documentary evidence and found that the cheques underlying the $276,876.65 claim were issued by Pioneer and signed by Xu and Zheng, not by Zhu, and that any claim on those cheques lay against Pioneer rather than Zhu. For the alleged unexplained withdrawals, the court observed that Xu’s affidavits did not address or substantiate the alleged 89 withdrawals totalling $744,737.42, and that there was no evidence linking Zhu to those transactions. Regarding the purchase order, payments to third parties, dealings with Lin Duan, and financial statements, the court found that Xu had not provided evidence of Zhu’s involvement or of any improper conduct, and that statutory provisions on financial statements did not create a civil right for Xu to compel Zhu to produce accounts outside an AGM. The court concluded that Xu had not discharged the burden of proof on any of his claims against Zhu and dismissed his claim in full. On the counterclaim, the court held that any claim for accounting fees or additional interest on a UOB loan belonged to Express Corporate Services Pte Ltd rather than to Zhu personally, and that there was no evidence connecting the alleged additional interest to Xu’s actions in bringing the suit. The court therefore dismissed the counterclaim and ordered that Zhu receive costs on the claim while Xu receive costs on the counterclaim.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Zhu Yimin

Facts.Text: Zhu Yimin runs a corporate services company, Express Corporate Services Pte Ltd, which is in the business of providing accountancy and secretarial services, and incorporating companies.
In 2016, at the request of Zheng Chunmu, Express Corporate Services Pte Ltd assisted with the incorporation of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd.
Ms Zhu was a director of Pioneer from 23 November 2016 to 25 September 2019, and from 30 July 2020 to 1 January 2021.
Ms Zhu was the sole director of Pioneer from 23 November 2016 to 27 May 2018, and from 15 January 2019 to 14 August 2019.
Ms Zhu was the sole director of Pioneer from 23 November 2016 to 27 May 2018, and from 15 January 2019 to 14 August 2019.
Ms Zhu was a director of Pioneer from 23 November 2016 to 25 September 2019, and from 30 July 2020 to 1 January 2021.
Ms Zhu was a director of Pioneer from 23 November 2016 to 25 September 2019, and from 30 July 2020 to 1 January 2021.
Ms Zhu was a director of Pioneer from 23 November 2016 to 25 September 2019, and from 30 July 2020 to 1 January 2021.
On 22 March 2018, ECS issued a tax invoice to Pioneer charging $1,200 for 'one year [of] nominee director fee' from 23 November 2017 to 22 November 2018.
The letter of appointment of Gao Jie, dated 14 May 2018, was signed by Ms Zhu.
On 18 October 2018, an email from Xiao Ru to 'Jason' in Pioneer sought information about bank statements, loan details, the recipients of certain payments and the purpose of such payments.
On 28 November 2018, ECS issued an invoice to Pioneer charging $80 for Ms Zhu going to the bank to sign as a director.
The certificate certifying that Lin Duan holds 30,000 shares in Pioneer has a signature that appears to be similar to Ms Zhu's.
On 18 December 2019, ECS issued an invoice to Pioneer for $2,660 for accounting services.

On 25 September 2019, Mr Xu asked Ms Zhu through WhatsApp for her help with a 'power of attorney for debt collection' for his suit with Mr Zheng; Ms Zhu said she would need Mr Xu to bring the bank statement and accounting documents and said, 'Just because you all said the other side took the money' 'I can't believe you'.

Around 8 July 2020, Ms Zhu told Mr Xu that she is a 'nominee director', that she provided '[j]ust service', was 'not [the] real boss' and maintained that 'I am different from you two [ie, Mr Xu and Mr Zheng]'.
Around 23 October 2023, interest on the loan to ECS from UOB went from 1.9% to 6.25% per year on a monthly basis.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on her pre-dispute role as a recorded director and provider of corporate services, and the documentary trail of signatures, invoices, and communications, Zhu Yimin could be held responsible to account for or repay sums relating to Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd’s cheques, withdrawals, purchase orders, payments, share issuances, cheque collections, and financial statements, and whether she could recover fees and additional interest said to be connected to services provided by Express Corporate Services Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: Under general company law principles, a person recorded as a director or service provider may be account or repay only if the claimant proves that the person exercised authority over the relevant company transactions or undertook obligations giving rise to personal liability, and a party seeking payment for services or consequential financial loss must it is the correct contracting party and that the services were rendered or the loss was caused by the opposing party’s conduct.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute records Zhu Yimin was recorded as a director of Pioneer Energy Holdings Pte Ltd during specified periods and that Express Corporate Services Pte Ltd invoiced Pioneer for nominee director services and accounting and tax services. The documents operational items such as cheques and authorisation letters were signed in the names of Zheng Chunmu and Xu Jinsong, while Zhu’s involvement appears in documents such as a staff appointment letter and a share certificate, and in communications where she requested bank statements and described herself as a nominee director without direct control over operations. The invoices for services and the UOB loan documentation identify Express Corporate Services Pte Ltd as the contracting entity, not Zhu personally. These pre-dispute facts frame the extent of Zhu’s formal roles and the separation between her personal capacity and the corporate capacity of Express Corporate Services Pte Ltd in relation to Pioneer’s transactions and the claimed fees and interest.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court examined whether Zhu Yimin functioned as a managing director or as a nominee director and concluded, based on WhatsApp messages, invoices, and ACRA records, that she was a nominee director with a non-executive role. It relied on communications in which Zhu described herself as a nominee director providing service and not the real boss, and in which Xu Jinsong described Zheng Chunmu as the actual controller responsible for operations and profit and loss. The court noted that Zhu and her firm had to request bank statements and payment information from Xu, indicating that she did not have direct access to Pioneer’s bank accounts. In relation to the cheques totalling $276,876.65, the court compared signatures and found that the cheques were issued by Pioneer and signed by Xu and Zheng, not by Zhu, and that there was no evidence she authorised or participated in those cheques; any claim lay against Pioneer, not Zhu. For the alleged unexplained withdrawals of $744,737.42, the court found that the claimants had not adduced evidence of the withdrawals or of Zhu’s involvement, and accepted Zhu’s evidence that only Xu and Zheng were signatories for Pioneer’s bank transactions. Regarding the purchase order PO180429, the court observed that the claimants had taken inconsistent positions about the signature and had not proved that Gao Jie signed it or that Zhu was involved, and therefore rejected the claim. The court found no evidence connecting Zhu to the $20,000 payment to Pan Yong or to any improper purpose, and similarly no evidence that she signed or issued the $1,070 cheque to WKS Welding Products Pte Ltd. On the dealings with Lin Duan, the court accepted that a share certificate bore a signature similar to Zhu’s but noted that ACRA records did not show Lin Duan as a shareholder and that the authorisation letter for cheque collection was signed by Zheng, not Zhu; there was no evidence of wrongdoing or of Zhu’s role in any cheque collection. For the period 30 July 2020 to 1 January 2021, the court accepted Zhu’s explanation that she was mistakenly filed as a director by her assistant and that she corrected the error with ACRA. On the request for financial statements, the court applied Ezion Holdings Ltd v Teras Cargo Transport Pte Ltd and held that the Companies Act provisions relied on by Xu create offences for failure to lay accounts at an AGM but do not support a civil claim to compel a director to produce accounts outside an AGM, and further noted that there was no evidence that AGMs had been held or that Zhu alone bore responsibility, given that Xu was also a director for part of the relevant period. Considering authorities such as Prima Bulkship and BIT Baltic, the court accepted that nominee directors owe baseline duties but found that the claimants had not proved any specific acts by Zhu that would amount to a breach of those duties. The court also noted that ACRA had informed Xu that his complaints did not disclose wrongdoing under the Companies Act. Accordingly, the court dismissed all claims against Zhu. On the counterclaim, the court held that the invoice for $2,660 was issued by Express Corporate Services Pte Ltd, not by Zhu personally, and that there was no evidence from Pioneer acknowledging the debt or evidence of services rendered, so the counterclaim was dismissed. For the alleged additional interest on a UOB loan, the court found that any loss would be that of Express Corporate Services Pte Ltd, that there was no evidence linking the increased interest rate or refinancing difficulties to the present suit, and dismissed that counterclaim as well. The court ordered that Zhu receive costs on the claim and that Xu receive costs on the counterclaim, with directions for submissions on quantum.
